Q1: My study routine was pretty structured, honestly. I'd dedicate around 30 hours a week, mostly in the evenings after work and other commitments. Being 26 and having been through a few years of university already, I know what works for me—I'd listen to lecture recordings and podcasts while commuting or doing chores, since I learn best through audio. I'd block out specific times, but I'll admit my actual engagement with the material varied even when I was technically 'studying.'
Q2: I tried to keep things regular, but life has a way of throwing curveballs. Generally, I'd study Monday through Friday evenings and do bigger chunks on weekends. When deadlines approached, I'd definitely ramp things up, but my baseline was pretty consistent. The regularity helped me manage stress—I've learned that cramming doesn't work well for me at this stage in my life.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or if grades had been posted. Sometimes I'd log in to download materials I needed for offline studying. I'd also check the discussion boards occasionally to see if anyone had questions similar to mine. Honestly, I wasn't logging in constantly—maybe a few times a week unless something specific was happening.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than something I naturally gravitated toward. I'd check it at the start of each week to see what was coming up, but it never became a daily habit. My routine was more about the actual studying than the platform itself—the LMS was just where I grabbed what I needed.
Q5: The recorded lectures were gold for me since I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times, adjust the speed, and really absorb the information. The discussion boards were also helpful when peers explained concepts in different ways—sometimes hearing someone else's take made things click. The peer interactions there actually supplemented my understanding quite a bit.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials posted as PDFs—I'd download them but rarely got through them thoroughly. Written resources just don't stick for me the way audio does. I also didn't engage much with some of the optional quizzes because they felt time-consuming for minimal payoff. My assignment completion rate reflects that I prioritized what I thought would directly impact my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to understanding the material or improving my exam performance. Anything that felt like busywork or checkbox exercises, I'd deprioritize. I also valued activities where I could interact with others—hearing different perspectives or explanations really helps me learn. If something was just reading and clicking through, I'd probably skim it.
Q8: Definitely different. Required activities had this weight to them—I knew I had to do them, so I'd schedule them in. Optional ones felt like nice-to-haves that I rarely got around to because something else always seemed more pressing. With medium stress levels and a lot on my plate, optional activities were the first things I'd cut when time got tight.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded most of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone during commutes and workouts. I also used external podcasts and YouTube videos to supplement topics I found confusing. A lot of my studying happened with headphones in, away from any screen that would log my activity. My notes were handwritten and I'd review those while listening to recordings.
Q10: Most of my meaningful studying happened offline. I'd download materials once, then spend hours listening and re-listening without any LMS interaction. When I was walking through campus or doing errands, I'd have lecture audio playing. That's when concepts really sank in for me, but there'd be zero trace of that in the system. My exam scores came from that invisible effort.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture video or audio file once, and that single access would turn into maybe five or six listens over the following week. The LMS would show I accessed it once for a few minutes, but I actually engaged with that content for hours. This was my primary study method—grab it once, use it repeatedly offline.
Q12: Around midterms, things got tough. I had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and some personal stuff going on. My motivation dipped—I was going through the motions but not really absorbing things. The mindfulness exercises actually helped me reset during that period. I never felt overwhelmed to the point of panic, but there were definitely low-energy weeks.
Q13: Fatigue was a big one. After long days, logging into the LMS felt like another chore, so I'd often just rely on materials I'd already downloaded. Competing responsibilities meant I'd sometimes prioritize other things over checking discussion boards or completing optional tasks. The LMS engagement suffered, but my actual studying continued through other means.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be on the LMS, scrolling through materials, but my mind was elsewhere. I'd watch a video but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was stressed about something else. Those sessions felt unproductive—I was technically engaged according to the system, but I wasn't actually learning. Quality of attention mattered more than time spent.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments more than I should have—my completion rate shows that. Even when I knew something was important, if it felt tedious or if I couldn't see immediate value, I'd push it back. My motivation being on the lower side meant I'd sometimes choose to do peer interactions or discussions instead of solo written work. I knew it would catch up with me, but in the moment, I'd avoid what felt draining.
Q16: Deadlines were my primary driver for LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached, submit things last minute, and then go quiet again. Without a deadline looming, I'd focus on my offline studying and only check the LMS occasionally. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but deadlines gave me the urgency my low motivation couldn't generate on its own.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though I wished there was more of it. When I got comments on assignments, I'd actually go back and review related materials. Positive feedback boosted my mood and made me more likely to engage. But when feedback was delayed or minimal, I'd lose momentum. The connection between my effort and the response I got really influenced my next steps.
Q18: The course structure was decent—having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan. But the heavy emphasis on written assignments didn't play to my strengths as an auditory learner. I participated more in discussions and peer interactions because those felt more natural. The structure supported some engagement but didn't fully accommodate different learning styles.
Q19: Having recorded lectures made engagement easier—that was crucial for me. But the number of online courses I was juggling made it harder to give this one my full attention. The task completion requirements felt overwhelming sometimes, especially when they were reading-heavy. A more varied approach with audio options for more content would have helped me stay engaged.
Q20: I think that could be someone exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or maybe struggling to figure out what's actually important. They might be logging in, watching videos, reading discussions, but then not knowing how to translate that into submitted work. Or they could be dealing with perfectionism or anxiety about turning things in. High activity doesn't always mean high clarity.
Q21: That's pretty much me, honestly. My LMS activity was low, but I passed with a decent exam score because my real studying happened offline. I'd listen to lectures repeatedly while doing other things, review notes I'd written by hand, and discuss concepts with classmates outside the platform. The LMS just doesn't capture that kind of engagement, so someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, how long they're on a page. That's useful for spotting who's completely absent. But it misses depth of engagement, offline studying, and whether someone's actually absorbing material. It definitely misses auditory learners like me who download once and study repeatedly. It captures presence, not understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, making connections, and caring about understanding it—not just completing tasks. It's when I find myself mulling over a concept while doing something else entirely, or when I want to discuss ideas with peers. Engagement is mental investment, not just time logged. You can be on a platform for hours and not be engaged at all.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to lectures while walking or exercising. It wouldn't capture the conversations with classmates over coffee where we worked through difficult concepts. It wouldn't reflect that I chose this course because I genuinely wanted to learn, even when my motivation flagged. My resilience in pushing through tough weeks, the mindfulness practices that kept me grounded—none of that shows up in click data.
